description: An iconic symbol and national treasure of Cambodia, the sugar palm tree has been beloved by people of this country for generations. Almost every part of it can be used. Fun fact: there is a famous Khmer saying about sugar palm trees that goes “Drop by drop, the bottle is filled”, referring to the slow drop of sweet palm sap that fills the pipe used to collect it, a process that may take half a day and requires immense patience to complete.

A favorite cultural snack eaten seasonally at the end of the dry season, the Java Plum is a fruit found on trees all across Cambodia. During the period of Khmer New Year, it is commonly eaten as a snack with dipping salts composed of salt, sugar, MSG, and chili peppers. Fun fact: compounds in the fruit can stain your tongue purple and blue.

With blossoms often mistaken for those of strawberry plants, the Jamaican Cherry tree has long been another popular snack of Cambodians. The trees are resilient and can often be found by roadsides, with blooms and fruit all year round. The fruit is sweet, comparable to the taste of cotton candy or caramel, with several thousands of tiny yellow seeds inside. Fun fact: Jamaican cherries have no relation to common cherries!

khmer: ចាន់គិរី​/ចាន់រុី
description: A common source of shade from the grueling sun, the Monkey Pod Tree is a more rurally-found tree in Cambodia. A massive tree commonly used as a local gathering place, it can reach up to heights of 15-25 meters, with canopy diameters of up to 61 meters. Timber and shade have been the most common uses for this tree. Fun fact: it directs water to its roots by folding up its leaves at night, allowing rainwater to fall straight down.

Its most popular use being in Khmer cuisine, the jackfruit is a resilient tree that thrives best in hot, tropical climates. Being able to produce up to 200-500 fruits per year, this tree’s uses have been prevalent and diverse in Cambodia, both in traditional medicine and in local cuisines. Fun Fact: this tree is renowned for its fruit, which are considered the largest tree-born fruit in the world!

A common sight across schools in Cambodia, the Blackboard tree is one that has contributed largely to the country’s education. Called so because of its popular use as wood for blackboards and whiteboards, this tree symbolizes knowledge, wisdom, and protection. Despite this, it has also been feared for its size and the saccharine sweet fragrance of its flowers, which have been believed to cause hallucinations or attract malevolent spirits. Fun fact: It goes by another name: “The Devil’s Tree”.

khmer: ជ្រៃស្លឹកតូច
description: European Boxwood (Buxus sempervirens) is a slow-growing, dense evergreen shrub native to Europe, North Africa, and Western Asia. In Cambodia, it is renowned for its ability to elevate gardens with its decorative perfection. Because of its pristine, enduring nature, boxwood is highly suitable for carving sacred iconography - such as Buddhas, devatas, and mythological creatures - allowing artisans to honor ancient Angkorian traditions in a refined, lasting way.

Renowned as the national flower of Cambodia, the rumduol flower is a flower that can be found almost everywhere in the country. Predominantly found around the ancient temples of Siem Reap Province, this flower gives off a sweet scent around its blooming months (December to April). It has been featured in carvings on temple walls for centuries and serves as a vital part of Cambodian history. Fun fact: Despite being mainly known for its flowers, the rumduol tree actually has fruit too!
